segments.s173-g97: پدر is the grammatical subject. پیر + سر together describe his head (literally 'old of head' = gray‑headed): پیر modifies سر and the copula بود (past 'was') follows to form 'was gray‑headed'. و coordinates the next description: برنا (young) + دلیر (brave) is a parallel nominal phrase describing the young man; the second clause omits an explicit copula (بود) by poetic ellipsis, a common feature in classical Persian.
segments.s173-g98: دهن جنگ را is the definite object with را marking 'the mouth of battle'. باز here means 'open' (not 'again') and is used with کرده (past participle 'done') to give a past meaning 'opened' (کرده functions as the perfective participle forming a past action). چو شیر is a simile placed after the verb phrase: 'like a lion.'
segments.s173-g99: ندانست is the past negative of دانستن ('he did not know'). کاو is an archaic/clitic form meaning 'that he' (introducing the subordinate clause), not a literal verb here (this sense is not listed in the provided options). باز modifies بیند, meaning 'again'. بیند is the classical present/subjunctive form used here with future/optative force ('would see' / 'might see'). پسر is the object 'son'.
segments.s173-g100: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of' introducing the cause (ز رفتن = 'because of the going/departure'). دلش ('his heart') is the subject of بود (past 'was'). زیر و زبر is an idiomatic paired phrase meaning 'upside down / in turmoil' (literally 'under and over'); the two elements are juxtaposed to express disturbance or overturning.
segments.s173-g9: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' پشت here refers to 'the back/behind' in a figurative sense (i.e. lineage or the line of someone), so ز پشت سیاوش = 'from the line/back of Siavash.' یکی شهریار is an indefinite nominal phrase: یکی ('a certain') precedes شهریار ('king') to mean 'a certain king.'
segments.s173-g10: هنرمند is an adjectival/noun description of the preceding شهریار ('skillful' or 'skilled one'); the conjunction و links that quality to the following prepositional phrase. از marks source/possession ('of/from') and گوهر نامدار is a genitival phrase where گوهر ('gem' / 'noble essence') is qualified by نامدار ('renowned'), so 'renowned for his noble essence.'
segments.s173-g11: از این تخمه literally 'from this seed/stock' — تخمه here means 'offspring/lineage.' The second از introduces a further specification: از گوهر کیقباد = 'of the (noble) essence of Ki-Qobad,' i.e. specifying the distinguished ancestor. The two از phrases stack to mark origin and pedigree.
segments.s173-g12: ز مادر = 'from (the) mother' (i.e. on his mother's side). سوی here means 'toward / of the side of' and introduces the source side (سوی تور = 'from Tur' or 'toward Turan'). دارد نژاد is a light-verb construction: دارد ('has, possesses') + نژاد ('lineage/descent') = 'he has descent/lineage.'
segments.s173-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' آید is third-person singular 'comes' (here used as historical/present narration). به ایران marks the destination ('to Iran') and پی + name (= پی فرخش) means 'after/in pursuit of Farrokhsh' — پی governs the person sought.
segments.s173-g14: ز چرخ = 'from the wheel/heaven/fate' (source or agency). آنچه is the relative pronoun 'that which/whatever.' پرسد is third-person singular 'asks' and دهد is 'gives'; پاسخش ('its answer') carries the possessive clitic referring back to the antecedent (the heavens or the question). The clause therefore says that whatever the heavens are asked they answer.
segments.s173-g15: میان را ('the middle' / 'the interval' / 'the breach') is the direct object marked by را. ببندد (third-person singular, classical/historic present) means 'he closes' or 'he seals.' به کین پدر is an instrumental/causal phrase 'with/for the vengeance of his father' (کین = vengeance, modified by پدر). The verb takes میان را as its object and the prepositional phrase gives motive.
segments.s173-g16: کند is the light/causative verb 'makes/does' here rendering 'causes to be' or simply 'will (do)'. کشور تور = 'the country/realm of Tur' (noun + possessor). زیر و زبر is a fixed poetic pair meaning 'under-and-over' i.e. 'upside down, in turmoil/overturned' (the two antonymous words convey overturning). The clause says he will wreak havoc/upset Tur's land.
segments.s173-g57: This is a noun phrase that groups the objects to be freed: 'زمین را' (the earth, marked with the definite-object/ topical marker را), modified by 'همان' ('that very/that same'), followed by the comitative phrase 'با سپهر بلند' ('with the lofty/heavenly sky'). Word order is poetic (topic first), so the whole phrase functions as the object/theme that the following verb clause will act upon.
segments.s173-g58: This is the verb phrase giving the action: 'به دست تو' ('by/through your hand' — به here marks the agent/instrument), followed by the future auxiliary 'خواهد' + infinitive 'گشادن' ('will open/free'). 'ز بند' is a prepositional phrase 'from bondage' (ز = from, بند = fetter). The subject is omitted (typical in Persian); the verb is future/auxiliary + infinitive indicating a future action accomplished through the addressee's hand.
segments.s173-g59: Two balanced clauses joined by 'و': 'به رنج است گنج' literally 'treasure is by toil' — 'به' functions instrumentally ('by/through'), 'رنج' is the instrument/cause, 'است' is the copula and 'گنج' is the logical predicate. The second clause 'به نام است رنج' inverts the order but parallels the first: 'رنج' is now the predicate and 'به نام' ('for/for the sake of name/fame') gives the motive or result. The inversion is a poetic device; verb/copula 'است' links the prepositional phrase with the noun.
segments.s173-g60: 'همانا' is a sentential particle ('verily/indeed') introducing an emphatic clause. 'که' here introduces the content clause 'that ...'. 'نامت' is 'your name/renown' (possessive -ت). 'به آید' uses the simple present/subjunctive form of آمدن ('to come') with the prefixed preposition 'به' (here functioning as part of the idiom 'به آید' = 'will come/arise'); 'ز گنج' = 'from treasure' (ز = from). The clause asserts that the person's fame will arise from wealth; the verb form is classical/poetic but functions as an assertive future/result.
segments.s173-g61: 'اگر' introduces the conditional clause. 'جاودانه' is an adjective 'everlasting/eternal'. 'نمانی' is the negative present/subjunctive (2nd person) of ماندن ('to remain'), i.e., 'you do not remain/you will not remain'; classical Persian uses this form in conditions. 'به جای' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in your place' or 'here' — the whole clause means 'if you do not remain forever in your place.'
segments.s173-g62: 'همی' is an adverb meaning 'still/yet' (here marking continuity). 'نام' = 'name/reputation' is the theme. 'به زین سپنجی' is a prepositional phrase 'on/from this cushioned/spongew-like saddle' (زین = saddle, سپنجی = spongy/cushioned) qualifying where the name will be celebrated. 'سرای' is a poetic verb form (from سرودن/سراییدن) meaning 'to sing/chant/celebrate' — here it functions as an assertion that the name will be chanted; the verb is classical/poetic in form and matches the subject 'نام' (the name will be sung/celebrated).
segments.s173-g63: 'جهان را' (world + object marker را) here marks the domain affected by the action; Persian poetic word order places it before the object and verb. 'یکی شهریار آوری' is an imperative/hortative construction where 'یکی' = 'one/a single', 'شهریار' = 'sovereign/king', and 'آوری' (from آوردن) is the 2nd/3rd-person present/subjunctive or jussive form used as an exhortation — 'bring/cause to appear'. Together: 'Bring one king to the world.'
segments.s173-g64: 'درخت وفا را' = 'the tree of fidelity/faithfulness' (را marks the direct object). The verb phrase 'به بار آوری' is an idiom: 'بار آوردن' means 'to make bear fruit / to cause to produce'; here 'به' introduces the result and 'بار' (literally 'load' or 'fruit/produce' in poetic usage) is what is borne. 'آوری' is the causative/bringing-about sense of آوردن, so the clause means 'cause the tree of fidelity to bear fruit' (i.e., make loyalty flourish).
segments.s173-g25: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; the clause follows a time‑before main clause order. از خواب is a prepositional phrase 'from sleep' (خواب = sleep) and گودرز is the subject. بیدار شد is a two‑word predicate: بیدار (awake) + شد (past of شدن, 'became'), so literally 'became awake' = 'woke.'
segments.s173-g26: نیایش کنان is a present/adverbial participle: نیایش 'prayer' + کنان 'doing' → 'praying' or 'while offering prayers.' پیش دادار شد is the result/main action: پیش 'in the presence of/before', دادار 'the Creator', and شد ('became' / simple past auxiliary) used idiomatically for 'went/appeared before.' The participle modifies the main verb, so the whole means 'he, praying, came before the Creator.'
segments.s173-g27: بمالید is past tense (3rd singular) 'he rubbed/pressed.' The prepositional phrase بر خاک 'on/to the earth' marks the surface, and ریش سپید 'white beard' is the thing rubbed — Persian omits a possessive ('his beard') in such contexts. Word order here puts the verb before the preposition and noun phrase, a poetic arrangement.
segments.s173-g28: ز is the poetic short form of از and here means 'because of' or 'by/from the action/support of'; شاه جهاندار is a compound title 'the king, lord of the world.' شد is past 'became' and پرامید is an adjective 'hopeful,' so the clause reads 'he became hopeful because of the king.'
segments.s173-g29: چو = 'when' introduces the temporal subordinate clause. خورشید پیدا شد is the predicate 'the sun became visible/appeared' (پیدا + شد). از پشت زاغ is a locative phrase 'from behind the crow/rook' (پشت = behind, زاغ = crow/rook) giving the point of emergence.
segments.s173-g30: برآمد is a simple past 'arose/issued forth.' به کردار is a prepositional expression meaning 'in the manner/form of' (کردار here 'form/manner'), and زرین چراغ is the simile 'golden lamp' (زرین = golden, چراغ = lamp), so the clause says the sun 'arose like a golden lamp.'
segments.s173-g31: سپهبد is the subject (a high military title). نشست is the past tense 'sat down' / 'took a seat.' The classical sequence از بر تخت عاج (literally 'from/on the ivory throne') here functions idiomatically to mark the place where he sat — i.e. he took his seat on the ivory throne (عاج = ivory).
segments.s173-g32: بیاراست is past 'he adorned' (active). ایوان is the object 'the iwan/porch.' به before کرسی ساج is instrumental/constructive ('with/by means of a saj seat'); کرسی 'seat/throne' modified by ساج (here the tree/wood type) gives 'a seat of saj wood.' The clause therefore says he furnished/adorned the iwan with a saj seat.
segments.s173-g33: پر اندیشه is an adverbial phrase meaning “full of thought/concern,” placed before the verb as in classical Persian. مر is an archaic enclitic for مرا (“me/to me”); گیو را پیش خواند is the main clause: پیش here means “before/in the presence of,” را is the definite/object marker, and خواند is the 3rd-person past tense used here in the sense “called/summoned.”
segments.s173-g34: وز آن خواب is an archaic/poetic link: وز functions like و از (“and/from that”) to introduce what follows; آن خواب = “that dream.” چندی is an adverb meaning “for a while,” سخنها is the plural “words/speeches,” and براند (3rd sing. past of راندن) is used bilding “put forth/uttered” (here: ‘spoke’). The verb comes at the end as typical in Persian.
segments.s173-g35: بدو is an indirect-object prepositional/adverbial ‘to him/unto him’ placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense ‘said’ (classical narrative) with the reported clause following. فرخ پی is an idiomatic pair meaning that good fortune (‘فرخ’) follows (‘پی’ = following) you; و روز تو continues the address: “and your day.” The clause is elliptical and continues into the next segment.
segments.s173-g36: همان اختر گیتی افروز تو is a predicative phrase with the copula omitted (common in Persian): همان = “that very,” اختر = “star,” گیتی‑افروز = a compound/phrase ‘world‑illuminating’ (افروز, present participial adjective), and تو is the addressed pronoun; read as “you are that very star who lights the world.”
segments.s173-g37: تو is the topical pronoun. تا زادی functions as a temporal phrase ‘from/as soon as (your) birth’—زادی is a noun from زاد ‘birth/origin.’ از مادر پاکدین specifies origin ‘from (your) mother, the pious one’; پاکدین is an epithet ‘pious’ used as a noun/adjective.
segments.s173-g38: پر از آفرین شد is a common construction: پر = “full (of),” از = “of,” آفرین = “acclamation/praise,” and شد is the past of شدن ‘became’ → “became full of praise.” سراسر زمین is an adverbial phrase ‘throughout the earth,’ placed after the verb phrase.
segments.s173-g39: به فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase ‘by the command of God’ (به = by/through, فرمان = command, یزدان = God). خجسته qualifies سروش (Soroush, the divine messenger/angel) as ‘auspicious/blessed’; syntactically the whole phrase is an instrumental/agent-like preface to the following verb.
segments.s173-g40: مرا روی بنمود breaks into مرا (me as object; here given as م + را) + روی (‘face’) + بنمود (3rd-singular past of نمودن, ‘showed/revealed/appeared’). در خواب دوش = ‘in the dream of last night’ (دوش = last night). Word order places the object before the verb, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s173-g1: چنان is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis. دید is the past tense 'saw/beheld' and precedes the subject گودرز (typical Classical Persian V+S order here). یک شب is a temporal phrase 'one night', and به خواب ('to/into sleep') is the idiom 'in a dream' — the whole phrase means 'he saw in a dream one night.'
segments.s173-g2: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. ابری (a cloud, note the -ی suffix forming the noun) is the subject and برآمد is the past 'rose/emerged'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and ایران names the source; پرآب is a compound adjective 'water‑full' (i.e. laden with water). Word order here is Subject + Verb + adjuncts.
segments.s173-g3: بر آن ابر باران is a locative phrase 'on/upon that rain‑cloud' (ابر باران = 'rain‑cloud'). خجسته is an adjective 'auspicious/blessed' modifying سروش (the divine messenger). The whole string names the speaking agent and its location: 'the auspicious Soroush on that rain‑cloud.'
segments.s173-g4: به گودرز is the indirect object 'to Goudarz'. گفتی is the poetic past 'said' (Ferdowsi often uses گفتی where modern Persian would use گفت), introducing reported speech. که introduces the quoted content, and بگشای is an imperative/subjunctive 'open' with گوش 'ear' meaning idiomatically 'listen.'
segments.s173-g5: چو here is a conditional 'if' (classical usage). خواهی is 'you wish/want' (2nd person). که introduces the subordinate clause and یابی is the 2nd‑person form 'you find' (used here in a subjunctive/optative sense: 'you may find' / 'you will find'). ز تنگی رها = 'free from narrowness' where تنگی means hardship/distress and رها functions as predicate 'free.'
segments.s173-g6: وز here is the poetic fused form representing 'و + ز' ('and from'); it connects the two sources of release. این نامور ترک names the enemy 'this famed Turk', and نر اژدها is an apposition 'male dragon' (nر modifies اژدها). The phrase lists the other thing one will be freed from.
segments.s173-g7: به توران is a locative 'in/at Turan' (classical uses of به can mark location). یکی = 'a certain/one', and نامداری نو = 'new renown' or 'newly renowned (person)'; است is the copula 'is/there is'. The clause asserts the existence of a newly famous person in Turan.
segments.s173-g8: کجا here functions as an identifying/relative particle ('namely' / 'whose'), linking to the following nominal phrase. نام آن شاه = 'the name of that king' and کیخسرو identifies the name (Kay Khosrow). است is the copula. Together: 'whose name is that king Kay Khosrow.'
segments.s173-g41: نشسته is a participial/adjectival form meaning “sitting” or “seated” and describes the agent of the main verb that follows. بر ابری is a prepositional phrase (بر + ابری) meaning “on a cloud”; پر از is the adjective phrase “full of” followed by از + noun to mark the thing it is full of (باد و نم = “wind and dew”). The whole segment is an adverbial/participial clause setting scene or manner.
segments.s173-g42: بشستی is a past-form verb referring to the same agent introduced by the participle (here used to mean ‘you made/caused or came to be’ in classical usage — in modern terms often read as ‘you sat/you caused’). جهان را uses را as the definite object marker (marking جهان as the direct object). سراسر ز غم is an adverbial/phrasing meaning “entirely/throughout with sorrow” (سراسر modifying the result; ز غم = “with/of sorrow” in poetic ز).
segments.s173-g43: م (here + را) together functions as the object pronoun مرا (“me”), with را separated in the word list; دید is simple past “saw” and و گفت links to reported speech (“and said”). این همه غم چراست is the quoted question: این همه غم = “all this grief,” and چراست is an interrogative “why is it?” (classical word order places the predicate-final چراست after the noun phrase).
segments.s173-g44: This is a continuation of the quoted question: جهانی is used for ‘the world’ (here as the subject), پر از = “full of” takes از + noun (کین) as its complement, و links the second quality (بی‌نم). The final چراست again asks “why is it so?” — the interrogative predicate follows the descriptive phrase in classical order.
segments.s173-g45: ازیرا here is an archaic/poetic connective meaning roughly “for” or “because” (introducing a causal/explanatory clause). که is the subordinating/relative particle “that/who,” and بی‌فر = “without farr” (loss of divine radiance/glory) modifies شاه. و برزست is a poetic perfect/past participle meaning “and was exalted/rose” describing the king; together the clause characterizes the king’s condition or origin.
segments.s173-g46: ندارد is the negative present/past-core verb meaning “does not have” (here present/literary sense). همی is a classical adverbial particle adding continuity or emphasis (“always/still/indeed”). راه شاهان is a genitive construction “the way/manner of kings” (راه = manner/way), and نگاه is the noun “look/gaze/appearance”; the clause says he lacks the kings’ manner and regal aspect.
segments.s173-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction “when” (classical usage). کیخسرو is the proper name (subject) and آید is the simple present/future classical form “comes.” ز توران زمین is a poetic prepositional phrase “from the land of Turan” (ز = from; زمین = land), so this segment is a temporal/location clause that sets the time/place for what follows.
segments.s173-g48: سوی + noun marks direction/target (“toward the enemies”). افگند is a past-tense/preterite verb here meaning “he threw/cast” or “brought upon.” رنج و کین are the direct objects “suffering and vengeance/hatred,” so the whole clause means he inflicted hardship and enmity upon his foes.
segments.s173-g81: مرا + دشت و کوه است یک چند جای is an agent/possessor construction where مرا (a contracted/poetic form meaning “to me / for me”) marks the experiencer/possessor; دشت و کوه است = “there are plains and mountains” (است used impersonally: ‘there is/are’). یک چند جای is a quantifying phrase ‘in several/a few places’ (یک چند = some, جای = place). Word order is older/poetic: object/possessor particle precedes the existence-clause.
segments.s173-g82: مگر here functions as ‘unless’/‘except that’; پیشم آید is a present/subjunctive-type clause meaning ‘comes before me / appears to me’ (پیشم = ‘in my presence / before me’, آید = ‘comes’). یکی رهنمای = ‘one (single) guide’. The clause gives the single condition that would alter the preceding statement.
segments.s173-g83: به + noun phrase is a vocative/recipient marker here: 'to/for the fortunate world‑champion' — a direct address or indication of destination. پیروزبخت is an epithet ‘fortunate/fortunate‑fated’ and جهان‌پهلوان is an honorific compound ‘world‑champion / great hero’. The phrase is condensed and honorific.
segments.s173-g84: نیایم is an archaic/poetic verbal form built on نیا(ی) + ـیم and here functions as a first‑person clause ('I come / I will come' with classical negative/optative nuance in some contexts); جز = 'except'; از = 'from/with'; شاد و روشن‌روان = a compound description 'joyful and bright‑souled' (two adjectives linked by و). The whole clause says the speaker will come to that hero only in the described state.
segments.s173-g85: تو = 'you' (vocative/imperative addressee). مر is a reduced/poetic form meaning 'for me' / 'on my behalf' (placed after تو for emphasis). بیژن خرد را = 'Bijan's wisdom' with را as the definite/object marker. در کنار = 'at the side / beside' (در + کنار forming the locative phrase). Word order places the dative/emphatic element (مر) early for emphasis.
segments.s173-g86: بپرور is an imperative or hortative verb 'nurture / cherish' (prefix بـ- + پرور). نگهدارش here is a causative/imperative verbal noun phrase 'keep/protect him' (lit. 'be his keeper/preserver'). از روزگار = 'from fate / from the trials of time' (از + روزگار). The two verbs form a short command: cherish him and protect him from fortune.
segments.s173-g87: ندانم is the negative first‑person present of دانستن 'I do not know' (classical contraction placing negative prefix on the verb). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; دیدار باشد = 'there is a meeting / a meeting will be' with باشد used in the classical subjunctive/optative sense; جز این = 'except this'. The clause expresses ignorance about any meeting beyond the one just mentioned.
segments.s173-g88: که again introduces a clause 'that/who'; داند is the present of دانستن 'he/she/it knows'; چنین = 'thus/so' or 'such a thing'; جز جهان‌آفرین = 'except the world‑creator' (i.e. no one but the Creator knows such matters). This short clause states that only the Creator can know such things.
segments.s173-g65: بدو is the prepositional clitic (be + 0) meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense 'said' (here treated as the reporting verb). گیو‌ای is a vocative: the name Giv plus the vocative particle -ای ('O Giv'); پدر is vocative 'father' addressed directly; بنده‌ام is 'I am your servant' with the bound pronoun -ام 'I' and the noun بنده as a humble self-reference.
segments.s173-g66: بکوشم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive/formal present ('I will strive / I shall try'); به رای تو is a prepositional phrase 'by/according to your counsel' (به + رای + enclitic تو); تا introduces the temporal clause 'as long as/until' and combines with زنده‌ام, where زنده is 'alive' and -ام is the 1st-person enclitic 'I' (= 'while I am alive').
segments.s173-g67: خریدارم is a nominal phrase with the 1st-person enclitic -م: 'I am buyer / I will take it.' این را is the accusative-marked demonstrative 'this' (را is the object marker); راگر (archaic/poetic fusion here) functions as the conditional 'if' and آید is the present/subjunctive 'it comes' (i.e. 'if it comes'); به جای is the locative 'to/into its place.'
segments.s173-g68: به introduces a purpose/attendant phrase 'to/with'; فرخنده نام is a noun phrase 'auspicious/fortunate name' (فرخنده qualifying نام); و coordinates the next phrase; پی here means 'following / in the company of' and رهنمای is 'guide' — together 'and following a guide' or 'with a guiding attendant.'
segments.s173-g69: به ایوان شد is a motion/result clause 'he went to / arrived at the iwan (portico)'; و connects clauses; ساز رفتن is a nominalized phrase 'preparations/gear for going' (ساز as 'means/device' with the infinitive رفتن) and گرفت is the past 'he took up/began' (i.e. 'he took up the preparations for leaving').
segments.s173-g70: ز خواب is a prepositional phrase 'from sleep / having woken from sleep'; پدر is the subject 'the father'; مانده is a participial/past-stative 'remained' or 'was left' describing his state; اندر + شگفت is the locative-phrase 'in astonishment / in wonder.'
segments.s173-g71: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'; خورشید رخشنده is the noun phrase 'the radiant sun' (noun + adjective); آمد پدید is a verbal phrase 'came forth / appeared' (آمد + پدید used together to mean 'appeared/became visible').
segments.s173-g72: زمین شد is 'the earth became' (شد = became); به سان is the fixed simile marker 'like/as'; گل شنبلید is the comparison term 'a (flower of) fenugreek' (گل + شنبلید), so the whole means 'the earth became like a fenugreek flower.'
segments.s173-g49: نبیند is an archaic negative present/potential form (n- + verb) meaning 'does not see' or 'may not see'. کس with this negation functions as 'no one'; او را is the definite object (را after the pronoun). ز + گردان marks origin or group ('from/among the troop'); نیو here is an epithet of the troop ('new' / a local epithet). Word order is verb first (poetic) followed by subject and prepositional phrase.
segments.s173-g50: مگر introduces an exception ('except'). نامور is an epithet ('renowned/famed') modifying the person named next. پور is the patronymic 'son (of)', followed by the father's name گودرز and then the subject's own name گیو; the sequence gives 'the famed Giv, son of Goudarz.'
segments.s173-g51: چنین کرد is a verb phrase 'thus did (it)', with a poetic inversion: the action 'کرد' is stated first. بخشش here is a noun 'a grant/gift' (what was done), and سپهر بلند ('the lofty/heavenly sky') is the agent; the adjective بلند follows the noun in typical poetic ordering.
segments.s173-g52: که introduces a result or purpose clause ('so that/that'). از تو literally 'from you' in older Persian functions as 'for you' or 'with regard to you.' گشاید is a present/optative verb 'opens/loosens' (may loosen), and غم و رنج بند is a noun phrase 'the bond/fetter of grief and suffering' where بند is 'bond/fetter.'
segments.s173-g53: همی before a past-tense verb is an emphatic/iterative particle here ('indeed/you have'), and نام is 'name/reputation' as direct object. جستی is the past form of جستن ('you sought/you gained' in poetic usage). میان دو صف is a locative phrase 'between the two ranks/rows' indicating where the renown was won.
segments.s173-g54: کنون 'now' sets the time. نام جاویدت = 'your eternal name' where جاوید is the adjective 'eternal' carrying the possessive -ت. آمد (came) plus به کف (into the palm) is an idiom meaning 'has come into your possession.' Word order places the adjective+possessive before the verb in this poetic formula.
segments.s173-g55: که starts the subordinate clause ('that' / 'for'). تا here means 'as long as.' در جهان مردم است و سخن is a nominal clause 'there are people in the world and (there is) speech' — a poetic way to say 'so long as people and speech exist.' است is the copula 'is/are.'
segments.s173-g56: چنین 'such/so' modifies نام 'name' (fame). هرگز + نگردد is a negative modality: 'will never become' or 'shall never be made'; نگردد is an archaic subjunctive/future of گشتن 'to become.' کهن is the predicate 'old/ancient,' so together 'such a name will never become old.'
segments.s173-g17: This is a causative clause: 'به دریای قلزم' (towards/into the Sea of Qulzum) gives the location, then 'به جوش آرد آب' is a light-verb construction meaning 'causes the water to boil' (آرد = causes/brings). Word order is locative → result phrase; the second به introduces the result phrase 'to a boil'.
segments.s173-g18: Verb-first poetic clause. نخارد is an archaic/poetic form built on خاریدن (to scratch / to be pricked) used idiomatically here with ن- to convey 'will not be rid of / will not escape'. 'سر' = 'head' (the person), followed by the cause 'از کین افراسیاب' = 'from Afrasiab's vengeance'. Literally: 'his head will not escape from Afrasiab's vengeance.'
segments.s173-g19: همه‌ساله (annually) is an adverbial fronting the clause. 'در جوشن کین' is a locative phrase meaning 'in/under the cuirass (armor) of vengeance' (جوشن modified by کین). بود is the past copula 'was'. The construction describes habitual/past annual state.
segments.s173-g20: Temporal phrase 'شب و روز' (night and day) precedes the locative 'در جنگ' (in battle). 'بر زین بود' literally 'was on the saddle' — an idiom meaning 'was mounted / in the field on horseback'. بود again is the past copula.
segments.s173-g21: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. 'گردان ایران' = 'the troop(s) of Iran' (جَرْدان as a unit), and 'و گردنکشان' coordinates 'and the proud/rebellious ones'. This phrase sets the group being considered.
segments.s173-g22: نیابد is the negative form (ن + یابد) of یابیدن 'to find/attain', here 'finds/obtains no one'. جز 'except' introduces the exception 'از گیو' = 'except from Giv'. The following phrase 'از او کس نشان' rearranges the constituents in a poetic order: 'از او' = 'from him', 'کس' = 'anyone', 'نشان' = 'token/mark' — together: 'who bore any token from him.' The clause as a whole means no one among those ranks had any trophy/mark from him, except Giv.
segments.s173-g23: چنین است is a formulaic 'thus it is' opening the sentence. فرمان is 'decree/command', and 'گردان سپهر' is a poetic collocation: گردان 'revolving/turning' modifies سپهر 'the heavens/sky' (i.e. 'the revolving heavens'). The phrase names the cosmic decree.
segments.s173-g24: بدو دارد = 'it holds/has for him' or 'it grants him' (بدو = to him; دارد = has/gives). 'از داد گسترده مهر' lists what is granted: از داد = 'from justice' or 'by justice', گسترده مهر = 'wide‑spread affection/favour' (گسترده modifying مهر). The phrase means the celestial decree bestows justice and far‑spreading favour upon him.
segments.s173-g89: تو is the addressed 2nd-person pronoun (you). پدرود is a noun meaning ‘farewell’ used predicatively with the imperative باش (be!) — literally “be (my) farewell.” The clause is imperative and is joined by و to the following imperative phrase; مرا is the object ‘me’ (here split in the word list as م + را) and یاد دار is an idiomatic imperative ‘keep/hold in memory’ (یاد = remembrance + دار as an imperative/forming a causative/possession‑type verb ‘have/keep’).
segments.s173-g90: روان را is the object phrase ‘the soul’ marked by the definite object marker را (روان = soul/spirit). ز درد من is a prepositional phrase ‘from my pain’ (ز = from/through, درد من = my pain), placed after the object. آزاد دار is an imperative construction literally ‘make (it) free’ or ‘set/keep free’ (آزاد = free; دار here functions as an imperative/causative ‘hold/make’). The whole clause is a direct command requesting release of the soul from the speaker’s affliction.
segments.s173-g91: چو + شویی forms a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' and شویی is 2nd‑person singular of شستن/شوییدن ‘to wash/bathe’ (here: ‘when you bathe/cleanse yourself’). ز بهر پرستش is a purpose phrase ‘for the sake of worship’ (ز بهر = for/for the sake of; پرستش = worship). رخان is an adverbial/epithet modifying the act (literally ‘loose/relaxed’/‘soft’), giving the manner or state associated with the washing/rites.
segments.s173-g92: به من is a beneficiary phrase ‘for me’ or ‘on my behalf’ (به = to/for + من = me). بر here marks direction/aim (‘toward’/‘to’), and جهان‌آفرین را is the definite object ‘the Creator’ marked by را. بخوان is an imperative ‘recite/cry out/invoke’ (بخوان from خواندن used in spoken/poetic imperatives). Combined, the clause instructs the addressee to address or invoke the Creator on the speaker’s behalf.
segments.s173-g93: مگر here is a modal/particle that can introduce a supposition or exception (in classical/poetic usage it often conveys something like 'perhaps' or 'unless'); باشدم is an archaic first‑person form meaning ‘may I be’/‘I might be’ (subjunctive/potential). دادگر رهنمای is a nominal phrase combining دادگر (‘just, righteous’ or ‘just judge/ruler’) with رهنمای (‘guide/adviser’) — together meaning ‘a just guide’ or ‘a just and guiding judge.’
segments.s173-g94: به نزدیک is a locative phrase ‘near’ or ‘close to’ (به + نزدیک), آن is the demonstrative ‘that,’ and نامور کدخدای is the nominal phrase ‘the renowned village‑headman’ (نامور = famed/renowned; کدخدای = village headman/local chief). Together this phrase would locate the speaker (or his hoped-for office) close to that famed chief.
segments.s173-g95: به فرمان is an instrumental/agentive phrase ‘by the command’ or ‘at the order.’ بیاراست is a participial/adjectival form meaning ‘well‑adorned’ or ‘arrayed’ (placed before the verb as a circumstantial qualifier). آمد برون is the simple past ‘came out/forth’ (آمد = came; برون = out/ forth). The sequence is typical: circumstance (به فرمان بیاراست) + past action (آمد برون).
segments.s173-g96: This is a noun phrase describing the father: پدر = father (subject); دل پر از درد = ‘a heart full of pain’ (دل = heart, پر = full (of), از + درد = of/with pain); و رخ پر ز خون = ‘and a face full of blood’ (رخ = face, پر = full, ز = poetic variant of از = of/from, خون = blood). The repeated pattern پر از/پر ز + noun expresses ‘full of X.’
segments.s173-g73: بیامد is a past‑tense, third‑person singular verb 'came' placed before its noun phrase (poetic word order). کمربسته is a past participle/adjectival phrase 'girded' describing the following name. گیو is the proper name (the sentence's subject) and دلیر is an additional epithet appended after the name — both are descriptive modifiers of the subject.
segments.s173-g74: یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective 'a certain' (here 'a'), introducing the noun phrase. بارکش is a noun 'pack‑bearer / beast of burden' modified by the following adjective. بادپایی is an attributive adjective 'fleet‑footed / swift‑running'. به + زیر together form an adverbial phrase 'beneath / down (to beneath)', giving the spatial relation; the whole phrase functions as a circumstantial phrase linked to بیامد (i.e. what came up under/for him).
segments.s173-g75: به گودرز گفت is a prepositional object plus past verb: به + proper name 'to Goudarz' followed by گفت, the past 'said'. ای جهان‌پهلوان is a vocative phrase ('O world‑hero') addressing Goudarz; ای marks direct address and جهان‌پهلوان is an honorific noun.
segments.s173-g76: This segment consists of three coordinating epithets joined by و ('and'). Each adjective (دلیر, سرافراز, روشن‌روان) further qualifies the vocative; their order is rhetorical (heightening praise) and they grammatically stand in apposition to the addressee.
segments.s173-g77: کمندی و اسپی is a coordinated subject 'a lasso and a horse'. The element rendered 'م' is the contracted/copulative form of 'است' (copula/existential) linking subject and predicate; را here is functioning within the phrase indicating the beneficiary/target ('for me' / the familiar object), and یار بس is the predicate 'a companion (یار) enough (بس)' — together meaning 'a lasso and a horse are enough companions for me.' (Classical Persian often compresses copula and pronominal elements.)
segments.s173-g78: نشاید is an impersonal negative modal 'it is not fitting / it ought not to be'. کشیدن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to lead/take/carry' (here 'to take/lead [me]'). ب + دان together function as the prepositional phrase 'to that' (classical spelling: 'بدان' = 'to that'), followed by مرز 'frontier/border'. کس is the indefinite subject 'anyone' (lit. 'someone'); the clause thus means 'it is not fitting that anyone should take [me] to that frontier.'
segments.s173-g79: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. مردم is the subject 'people'. برم here functions as a relational/oblique element tied to 'مرا'/'for me' (classical compressed form signaling 'for me' or 'toward me') and is part of the expression meaning 'ask for me'. خواستار is an adjective/participial 'desirous / asking for', and آیدم is first‑person present/future 'I will come / I come' — together: 'when people come asking for me, I will come.'
segments.s173-g80: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / from then on'. مگر here means 'except / only if' (conditional restriction). کارزار is 'battle / warfare', and آیدم again is 'I will come'. The clause therefore restricts future coming: 'after that, I will come only if battle arises.'

sentences.s173-s49: The father was gray‑headed, and the young man brave; he opened the mouth of battle like a lion.
sentences.s173-s50: He did not know that he would see his son again; because of his son's departure his heart was all upset.
sentences.s173-s5: From the line of Siavash came a certain king, skillful and famed for his noble nature.
sentences.s173-s6: From this stock — from the noble essence of Kay Qobad — he traces his descent on his mother's side to Tur.
sentences.s173-s7: When he comes to Iran in pursuit of Farrokhsh, the heavens give answer to whatever is asked.
sentences.s173-s8: He will close the breach with vengeance for his father and will overturn Tur's country.
sentences.s173-s29: By your hand that very earth, together with the high heavens, will be freed from its bonds.
sentences.s173-s30: Treasure comes by toil, and toil (is gained) for a name; indeed, your renown will come from treasure.
sentences.s173-s31: If you do not remain forever in your place, still your name will be chanted from this cushioned saddle.
sentences.s173-s32: Bring one sovereign into the world; cause the tree of fidelity to bear fruit.
sentences.s173-s13: When Goudarz woke from (his) sleep, he went before the Creator, praying.
sentences.s173-s14: He pressed his white beard to the earth; because of the sovereign king he became hopeful.
sentences.s173-s15: When the sun appeared from behind the crow, it rose like a golden lamp.
sentences.s173-s16: The spahbed took his seat upon the ivory throne and adorned the iwan with a saj (wood) seat.
sentences.s173-s17: Full of thought, he summoned Giv before me, and from that dream he spoke for a while.
sentences.s173-s18: He said to him, “Good fortune follows you, and your day — you are that very star that lights the world.”
sentences.s173-s19: From the moment you were born of your pious mother, the whole earth was filled with praise.
sentences.s173-s20: By God’s command the auspicious Soroush showed me his face in a dream last night.
sentences.s173-s1: Thus Goudarz saw one night in a dream that a cloud rose from Iran, full of water.
sentences.s173-s2: Upon that rain-cloud the auspicious Soroush said to Goudarz, 'Open your ear.'
sentences.s173-s3: If you wish, you will find release from hardship and from this famed Turk, the male dragon.
sentences.s173-s4: In Turan there is a certain newly renowned man — his name is that king Kay Khosrow.
sentences.s173-s21: Sitting on a cloud full of wind and dew, you filled the world entirely with sorrow.
sentences.s173-s22: He saw me and said, “Why is there all this grief? Why is the world full of hatred and tastelessness?”
sentences.s173-s23: For (or: because) the king was without divine glory and exalted rank, he lacks the royal bearing and look.
sentences.s173-s24: When Kai Khosrow comes from the land of Turan, he casts suffering and vengeance toward his enemies.
sentences.s173-s41: There are plains and mountains for me in several places, unless a single guide comes before me.
sentences.s173-s42: To the fortunate world‑champion I will come only with a joyful, bright spirit.
sentences.s173-s43: You, for my sake, keep Bijan’s wisdom at your side; nurture him and spare him from the vicissitudes of fate.
sentences.s173-s44: I do not know that there will be any meeting except this; who knows such things but the Creator of the world?
sentences.s173-s33: Giv said to him, “O father, I am your humble servant; I will strive according to your counsel as long as I live.”
sentences.s173-s34: I will be its taker if it comes to its place, to an auspicious name and following a guide.
sentences.s173-s35: He went out onto the iwan and took up the preparations to depart; the father, roused from sleep, remained in astonishment.
sentences.s173-s36: When the radiant sun appeared, the earth became like a fenugreek flower.
sentences.s173-s25: No one among the troop sees him, except the famed Giv, the son of Goudarz.
sentences.s173-s26: Thus did the lofty heavens grant, so that the bonds of sorrow and hardship would be loosed from you.
sentences.s173-s27: You have won renown between the two ranks; now your eternal name has come into your hand.
sentences.s173-s28: As long as there are people and speech in the world, such a name will never grow old.
sentences.s173-s9: He made the waters of the Sea of Qulzum boil; Afrasiab's hatred would not spare his head.
sentences.s173-s10: Each year he wore the armor of vengeance; night and day he was in battle, mounted on his saddle.
sentences.s173-s11: From the ranks of Iran and the proud rebels he found no one—except Giv—who bore any token from him.
sentences.s173-s12: Thus is the decree of the revolving heavens: it grants him justice and wide‑spread favour.
sentences.s173-s45: Be the bearer of my farewell and keep me in your memory; free my soul from my suffering.
sentences.s173-s46: When you have bathed for the sake of worship, call upon the Creator for me.
sentences.s173-s47: Perhaps I may be a just guide.
sentences.s173-s48: At the command he came forth, well attired; the father, his heart full of pain and his face stained with blood.
sentences.s173-s37: Giv the brave came, girded; a swift‑footed pack‑beast came up beneath him.
sentences.s173-s38: He said to Goudarz, 'O world‑hero, brave and triumphant and bright‑souled!'
sentences.s173-s39: A lasso and a horse are enough companions for me; it is not fitting that anyone should carry me to that frontier.
sentences.s173-s40: When people come asking for me, I will come; thereafter I will come only if battle arises.
